reasoning: Candidate vfx_hit_spark_v06 is a large cool-blue/white starburst explosion occupying roughly 3-4x the footprint of the largest peer sprite. Peer set is Denzi-style character/creature sprites rendered in warm earthy browns, greens, reds, and muted purples with consistent 1px dark outlines and subtle internal shading. The candidate shares almost none of this visual DNA. (1) Outline weight/color: peers uniformly outlined, candidate none — expected for VFX but amplifies disconnection. (2) Shadow direction/count: peers 0-1 light drop-shadows; candidate zero — minor. (3) Highlight intensity: CRITICAL FAIL — candidate has a blown-out white-hot core radiating to yellow that is visually 5-6x brighter than any highlight on any peer; this alone reads as a different game engine. (4) Color temperature: candidate is predominantly cold blue (#7ecfff range) with warm yellow accents, while peers are warm-first with cool accents; temperature polarity is reversed. (5) Detail density: large chunky dithered pixel blocks vs. fine per-pixel work on peers at equivalent canvas area. (6) Palette overlap: near-zero — the electric blue and pure white that dominate the candidate appear nowhere in the peer sheet. (7) Proportion: not applicable (VFX), but scale disparity makes the candidate visually dominant in any composition alongside peers. (8) Rendering style: glow/particle burst vs. classic flat-shaded sprite. (9) CHARM LEVEL: peers exhibit cute RPG charm; candidate is a sterile explosion with no personality cues — a particularly damning gap given the project's Echo aesthetic brief. Even granting full exemption for 'different item type within category,' the palette, temperature, highlight intensity, and charm failures are style-intrinsic and cannot be explained by VFX-vs-character differences alone.
